text.fullText: S TATE OF M ICHIGAN DEPARTMENT OF ENVIRONMENT, GREAT LAKES, AND ENERGY W ATER R ESOURCES D IVISION GRETCHEN WHITMER PHILLIP D. ROOS GOVERNOR DIRECTOR February 17, 2026 VIA E-MAIL Lee Zeldin, Administrator U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Office of the Administrator OW-Docket@epa.gov Dear Lee Zeldin: Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929: Comments on the Proposed Rule Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations The Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy (EGLE), Water Resources Division, submits the following comments regarding the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (USEPA) proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 rule published in the Federal Register on January 15, 2026. EGLE is the certifying authority for the State of Michigan under CWA Section 401. Clean water is a critical issue to the State of Michigan. Michigan’s public health and economy, including tourism, agriculture, and major industries, are dependent on the Great Lakes and clean water. EGLE supports the provisions of CWA Section 401 as the basis for building a collaborative state and federal partnership in Michigan and other states to protect and improve water quality and support the designated uses of Michigan’s Great Lakes and surface waters. The current activity-based implementing regulations found at 40 CFR Part 121 allow the State of Michigan and federal agencies to realize the positive potential of this partnership under the principles of cooperative federalism and exercise the state authorities instilled by Congress to ensure that discharges authorized by federal agencies comply with state law. EGLE does not support the proposed update of the rule. Confining the scope of certification, limiting the type of conditions included in the certification, and other proposed changes are unnecessary and will have negative consequences for the health of the nation’s waters. Moreover, EGLE believes the 30-day comment period is inadequate. The current CWA Section 401 rule is effective and works well in Michigan. EGLE has not experienced problems in the implementation of the activity-based scope of certification since the current regulations took effect, and maintains there is not a compelling reason to revise the current rule. The opening sentence in CWA Section 401(b) says “Nothing in this section shall be construed to limit the authority of any department or agency pursuant to any other provision of law to require compliance with any applicable water quality requirements.” Section 401(d) follows that broad statement DEBORAH A. STABENOW BUILDING • 525 WEST ALLEGAN STREET • P.O. BOX 30458 • LANSING, MICHIGAN 48909-7958 Michigan.gov/EGLE • 800-662-9278 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Page 2 February 17, 2026 by mandating, in part, “Any certification provided under this section shall set forth any effluent limitations and other limitations, . . . and with any other appropriate requirement of State law set forth in such certification, and shall become a condition on any Federal license or permit subject to the provisions of this section.” Such clear language indicates that certification conditions may be based on appropriate state and tribal water quality requirements applicable to any water quality impacts from an activity within the breadth of the CWA’s subject matter. Narrowing the scope of certification and leaving other sources of water pollution from an activity uncontrolled will result in harm to the quality of the nation’s waters and is contrary to the purpose of the CWA. EGLE also does not support many of the other proposed changes, for example, replacing the term “project proponent” with the term “applicant” and limiting state and tribal water quality requirements to numeric standards or monitoring requirements. As implied in the Preamble, implementing such changes could be argued to exclude federal agency projects and the issuance of federal general licenses and permits from the 401 rule. A final rule that requires federal general license and permit applicants to seek individual certification would subject thousands of Michigan residents, small businesses, and others to new delays and regulatory burdens annually. The consequences of these and other proposed changes are significant and substantial and would harm the current, successful state and federal cooperative approach toward protecting water quality under the CWA framework. The activity-based CWA Section 401 rule embodies a sincere and effective application of the principles of cooperative federalism. It has been in effect for more than two years without significant implementation challenges. Any present or future implementation problems should be addressed by USEPA through the provision of technical assistance to federal agencies, certifying authorities, or applicants pursuant to CWA Section 401(b), as opposed to finalizing the proposed rule. If you have questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 517-331-7966; LoundsA@Michigan.gov; or EGLE, P.O. Box 30458, Lansing, Michigan 48909-7958. Sincerely, Amy Lounds, Assistant Division Director Water Resources Division cc: Anne Garwood, EGLE Lee Schoen, EGLE Matt Smar, EGLE Doug Bridges, EGLE
